content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street
Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
January 28, 2000
Jack L. White
City Attorney
City of Anaheim
200 South Anaheim Boulevard, Suite 356
Anaheim, California 92805
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-291
Dear Mr. White:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Tom Tait regarding the
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
May Council member Tait participate in decisions concerning the Gotcha Glacier project
on which Turner Construction is a contractor, given that Council member Tait’s civil engineering
firm has submitted a bid, together with Turner Construction and others, on an unrelated county
project, and therefore Turner Construction may be a source of income to him in the future?
CONCLUSION
Under the Act, Council member Tait may not maice, participate in making, or in any way
attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on his engineering firm or a
client of his firm that has paid the firm $500 or more. Turner Construction is not currently a
source of income to Council member Tait’s engineering firm and it has not been a source of
income to the firm in the past 12 months. The fact that Council member Tait’s firm is involved
Government Code sections 81000
- 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-99-291
Page 2
with Turner Construction in a bid on a different project, and if successful in the bid, might
potentially receive payments from Turner Construction does not disqualify Council member Tait
from voting on the Gotcha Glacier project for which Turner Construction is general contractor at
this time. If the bid submitted by Turner Construction, Council member Tait’s firm, and others
for work on the county project is successful, and Turner Construction becomes a source of
income or promised income to Council member Tait’s engineering firm in the future, he would
then be disqualified from decisions affecting Turner Construction.
FACTS
The City of Anaheim has entered into a ground lease with Glacier of Anaheim, LLP
(“Glacier”) to construct and operate a sports-oriented entertainment project and related uses on
an approximately 6.5-acre parcel of land owned by thç city adjacent to Edison International Field
of Anaheim. The Gotcha Glacier project is billedas America’s first indoor snowboard and surf
park. The project will require discretionary entitlements and approvals from the city council.
2
Glacier has a development agreement with Kajima Urban Development LLC (“Kajima”)
to act as Glacier’s representative in the construction of the project and to deliver the completed
project to Glacier at a fixed price of approximately $104 million. Kajima has a service contract
with Turner Construction Company, Inc. (“Turner Construction”) and is currently in the process
of executing a construction contract with Turner for construction of the project for approximately
$70 million. Turner Construction is a very large construction company. You state that it is
reasonably foreseeable at this time that Turner will be the general contractor hired to construct
the project if it is built following approval by the city, and that the decisions of the city council
concerning the project will have a material financial effect upon Turner.
Council member Tait owns a 50 percent interest in a civil engineering firm, Tait and
Associates (the “firm”). Tait and Associates has participated with others, including Turner
Construction, in submitting a proposal to the County of Orange relating to a proposed county
project. The county project involves the design and construction of use facilities. The project
will be competitively bid based on qualifications and there will be several bidders. If the
proposal is approved, the county will enter into a construction contract with Turner Construction.
Either Turner Construction will directly enter into a subcontract with Tait and Associates for
necessary engineering services for the project, or, more likely, the project architect will enter into
2 The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. This advice is applicable and
confers immunity (see Section 83114) only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct and that all of the
material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, 77.) Please note that nothing in this
letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. The Commission will not
advise with respect to past conduct, such as votes that have already taken place. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)


File No. A-99-291
Page 3
a subcontract with the firm for such services. It is not known at this time whether the firm will
be compensated by Turner Construction or the architect.
3
ANALYSIS
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
fmancial interests ofpersons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.
A public official has a “financial interest” in a governmental decision, within the meaning
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more ofthe public official’s economic interests. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision. (Regulation
1 8700(b)(1)-(8).)
1. Public official.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to “public officials.” (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) As a member of the Anaheim City Council, Mr. Tait is
a “public official,” for purposes ofthe Act (see Sections 82048, 82041), and the conflict of
interest rules apply to him.
2. The Act’s conflict-of-interest rules apply to this decision.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official “make[s],
participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.”
(Section 87100; Regulation 18700(b)(2).) The Commission has adopted a series ofregulations
which define “malcing,” “participating in making,” and “influencing” a governmental decision,
and which provide certainexceptions. (Regulations 18702-18702.4.) In this case, the Anaheim
City Council will be malcing decisions on discretionary entitlements and approvals necessary for
construction of the Gotcha Glacier project.
The facts in the incoming request for advice were supplemented by telephone conversations with
Ms. Selma Mann of the Anaheim City Attorney’s office.


File No. A-99-291
Page 4
3. Identifying economic interests.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from economic
interests. The economic interests from which conificts of interest may arise are defined in
Regulations 18703-18703.5. Identifying which, if any, of these economic interests are held by a
public official is the third step in analyzing a potential conflict of interest under the Act. (See
Regulation 18700(b)(3).)
There are five kinds of such economic interests:
•
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment
4 of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds anyposition ofmanagement (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b));
•
A public official has an economic interest in realproperty in which he or she has a direct
or indirect interest of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);
•
A public official has an economic interest in any source ofincome, includingpromised
income, which aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);
•
A public official has an economic interest in any source ofgifts to him or her ifthe gifts
aggregate to $300 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4);
•
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets,
or liabilities, as well as those ofhis or her immediate family—this is known as the
“personal financial effects” rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).
Council member Tait’s civil engineering firm is an economic interest ofhis, because he
has an investment ofmore than $1,000 in the finn, he holds a position ofmanagement in the
firm, and the firm has been a source of income to him of $250 or more in the past 12 months.
(Section 87103 (a), (c) and (d).) The Act’s definition of “income” also provides that the income
of an individual includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which
the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater.
(Section 82030.) Since council member Tait owns a 50 percent interest in the engineering firm,
‘ An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent
child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the
official, the official’s agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent
interest or greater. (Section 87103.)


File No. A-99-291
Page 5
any client of the firm that has paid the firm enough that Mr. Tait’s one-half share of the income is
$250 or more is considered an economic interest of his.
Thus, Council member Tait may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt
to use his official position to influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect on his engineering finn or a client of his
firm that has paid the firm $500 or more.
Your question centers on whether Turner Construction would be considered a source of
income to Council member Tait, because it bid together with Council member Tait’s engineering
firm on an unrelated county project. His engineering firm has participated with others, including
Turner Construction, in submitting a proposal to the County of Orange relating to a proposed
county project. Ifthe proposal is approved, the county will enter into a construction contract
with Turner Construction. Either Turner Construction will directly enter into a subcontract with
the firm for necessary engineering services for the project, or the project architect will enter into
a subcontract with the firm for such services. It is not known at this time whether the finn will
be compensated by Turner Construction or the architect.
The fact that Council member Tait’s firm has submitted a bid together with Turner
Construction and other parties, on a county project, does not mean Turner Construction is
presently an economic interest of Council member Tait’ s finn. You stated that Turner
Construction is not presently a source of income to Council member Tait’s engineering firm, and
has not been a source of income to the firm in the past 12 months. As to the county project, no
bid has yet been accepted by the County of Orange. Further, it is not clear at this time whether
Council member Tait’s firm would enter into a subcontract with and be compensated by Turner
Construction or the project architect. Though Turner Construction may be a potential source of
income to Council member Tait’ s engineering firm, it is not presently a source of income to the
firm, nor is it a source ofpromised income to him. Thus, Turner Construction is not presently an
economic interest of Council member Tait’s. His engineering firm, Tait and Associates, is an
economic interest of his, and Turner Construction may be an economic interest ofhis in the
future if it becomes a source of income to his firm of $500 or more.
4.
Determining whether the public official’s economic interest is directly or
indirectly involved in the governmental decision.
The fourth step in analyzing a potential conflict of interest is to determine whether the
public official’s economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision
at issue. (Regulation 1 8700(b)(4).) This step is important because it helps determine (in the fifth
step) which test for materiality to use in deciding whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have a material financial effect on the economic interests.


File No. A-99-291
Page 6
A business entity, a source of income, or a source of gifts is directly involved in a
decision when that person:
•
Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request (Regulation 18704.1 (a)( 1));
•
Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official’s agency. A source of income is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity.
(Regulation 18704.1 (a)(2).)
Under the Commission’s regulations, sources of income which are not directly involved
under the rules stated above are considered indirectly involved for purposes of choosing a
materiality standard. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)
Council member Tait’s engineering firm is not directly involved in Council decisions
about discretionary entitlements and approvals necessary for construction and operation ofthe
Gotcha Glacier project. Tait and Associates might only be indirectly affected by such decisions.
5. Deciding which materiality standards to use to decide if there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect.
Because decisions about the Gotcha Glacier project may only affect Tait and Associates
indirectly, the indirect materiality standards ofregulation 18705.1(b) would apply. You have
stated that given the fmancial size of Tait and Associates, the applicable materiality standards are
those for a small business in regulation 18705.1(b)(7). That subdivision provides that the
financial effects of a govrnmental decision are material if it is reasonably foreseeable that any of
the following are true as a result of the decision:
“(A) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
gross revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or
(B) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(C) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more.” (Regulation
18705. l(b)(7)(A)-(C).)
In the terms ofthe present situation, the specific question is this: is it reasonably
foreseeable that the council decisions concerning the Gotcha Glacier project will result in any of
these materiality standards or tests being met as to Council member Tait’s engineering firm?
(Regulation 18706.)


File No. A-99-291
Page 7
6. Using the materiality standards to decide if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect.
The sixth, and usually most important step, in deciding whether you have a conflict of
interest is using the materiality standards (from step 5, above) to decide if a material financial
effect on one of more of your economic interests is reasonably foreseeable as a result ofthe
decision. (Regulation 18706.)
As used here, “reasonably foreseeable” means “substantially likely.” (Regulation 18706;
In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences of a governmental
decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made is highly situation-specific;
making this evaluation is a ‘judgment call.” A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the
standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. (Ibid.)
Because, Turner Construction has not been a source of income to Council member Tait’s
firm in the past 12 months, he may participate in council decisions about the Glacier project
unless it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would affect his engineering firm’s gross
revenues by $10,000 or more in a fiscal year; expenses by $2,500 or more in a fiscal year; or
assets or liabilities by $10,000.
For example, is it substantially likely that decisions to proceed with the Gotcha Glacier
project, for which Turner is acting as general contractor under a $70 million dollar contract, will
affect Council member Tait’s firm by the thresholds listed above? Decisions on the Glacier
project would obviously not affect the selection of Tait’s firm as a subcontractor on the county
project, as it has already been selected and included in the bid. Further, given that Turner
Construction is a very large construction company, it is doubtful that decisions about the Gotcha
Glacier project would affect Turner Construction’s financial viability or its ability to pay Council
•member Tait’s firm on the county project, if it were successful on the bid and if Turner
Construction entered into the subcontract with Tait and Associates instead of the architect. Thus,
it does not appear likely that decisions on the Gotcha Glacier project would have the requisite
financial effect on Council member Tait’s engineering firm. However, this is ultimately a
determination you must make based on all the facts.
However, as discussed above, if Turner Construction becomes a source of income to
Council member Tait’s engineering firm, then Turner Construction is an economic interest ofhis,
and Council member Tait will have to recuse himself from decisions about the Glacier project.


File No. A-99-291
Page 8
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Please feel free to contact the Commission for advice if you have questionsabout similar
situations in the future. For your information, we have included copies of section 87100.1 of the
Act and regulation 1 8702.4(b)(4), which, though not applicable in this case, relate to professional
engineers and might be of interest to you in advising Council member Tait in the future.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
ç&P IH
By:
Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Commission Counsel

value of assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more.” (Regulation

decision will have a material financial effect.
The sixth, and usually most important step, in deciding whether you have a conflict of
interest is using the materiality standards (from step 5, above) to decide if a material financial
effect on one of more of your economic interests is reasonably foreseeable as a result ofthe
decision. (Regulation 18706.)
As used here, “reasonably foreseeable” means “substantially likely.” (Regulation 18706;
In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences of a governmental
decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made is highly situation-specific;
making this evaluation is a ‘judgment call.” A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the
standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. (Ibid.)
Because, Turner Construction has not been a source of income to Council member Tait’s
firm in the past 12 months, he may participate in council decisions about the Glacier project
unless it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would affect his engineering firm’s gross
revenues by $10,000 or more in a fiscal year; expenses by $2,500 or more in a fiscal year; or
assets or liabilities by $10,000.
For example, is it substantially likely that decisions to proceed with the Gotcha Glacier
project, for which Turner is acting as general contractor under a $70 million dollar contract, will
affect Council member Tait’s firm by the thresholds listed above? Decisions on the Glacier
project would obviously not affect the selection of Tait’s firm as a subcontractor on the county
project, as it has already been selected and included in the bid. Further, given that Turner
Construction is a very large construction company, it is doubtful that decisions about the Gotcha
Glacier project would affect Turner Construction’s financial viability or its ability to pay Council
•member Tait’s firm on the county project, if it were successful on the bid and if Turner
Construction entered into the subcontract with Tait and Associates instead of the architect. Thus,
it does not appear likely that decisions on the Gotcha Glacier project would have the requisite
financial effect on Council member Tait’s engineering firm. However, this is ultimately a
determination you must make based on all the facts.
However, as discussed above, if Turner Construction becomes a source of income to
Council member Tait’s engineering firm, then Turner Construction is an economic interest ofhis,
and Council member Tait will have to recuse himself from decisions about the Glacier project.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Council member Tait participate in decisions concerning the Gotcha Glacier project
on which Turner Construction is a contractor, given that Council member Tait’s civil engineering
firm has submitted a bid, together with Turner Construction and others, on an unrelated county
project, and therefore Turner Construction may be a source of income to him in the future?

CONCLUSION: Under the Act, Council member Tait may not maice, participate in making, or in any way
attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on his engineering firm or a
client of his firm that has paid the firm $500 or more. Turner Construction is not currently a
source of income to Council member Tait’s engineering firm and it has not been a source of
income to the firm in the past 12 months. The fact that Council member Tait’s firm is involvedPage 2
with Turner Construction in a bid on a different project, and if successful in the bid, might
potentially receive payments from Turner Construction does not disqualify Council member Tait
from voting on the Gotcha Glacier project for which Turner Construction is general contractor at
this time. If the bid submitted by Turner Construction, Council member Tait’s firm, and others
for work on the county project is successful, and Turner Construction becomes a source of
income or promised income to Council member Tait’s engineering firm in the future, he would
then be disqualified from decisions affecting Turner Construction.

White City Attorney City of Anaheim 200 South Anaheim Boulevard, Suite 356 Anaheim, California 92805 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-99-291 Dear Mr. White: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Tom Tait regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ QUESTION May Council member Tait participate in decisions concerning the Gotcha Glacier project on which Turner Construction is a contractor, given that Council member Tait’s civil engineering firm has submitted a bid, together with Turner Construction and others, on an unrelated county project, and therefore Turner Construction may be a source of income to him in the future? CONCLUSION Under the Act, Council member Tait may not maice, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on his engineering firm or a client of his firm that has paid the firm $500 or more. Turner Construction is not currently a source of income to Council member Tait’s engineering firm and it has not been a source of income to the firm in the past 12 months. The fact that Council member Tait’s firm is involved Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18996, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-99-291 Page 2 with Turner Construction in a bid on a different project, and if successful in the bid, might potentially receive payments from Turner Construction does not disqualify Council member Tait from voting on the Gotcha Glacier project for which Turner Construction is general contractor at this time. If the bid submitted by Turner Construction, Council member Tait’s firm, and others for work on the county project is successful, and Turner Construction becomes a source of income or promised income to Council member Tait’s engineering firm in the future, he would then be disqualified from decisions affecting Turner Construction. FACTS The City of Anaheim has entered into a ground lease with Glacier of Anaheim, LLP (“Glacier”) to construct and operate a sports-oriented entertainment project and related uses on an approximately 6.5-acre parcel of land owned by thç city adjacent to Edison International Field of Anaheim. The Gotcha Glacier project is billedas America’s first indoor snowboard and surf park. The project will require discretionary entitlements and approvals from the city council. 2 Glacier has a development agreement with Kajima Urban Development LLC (“Kajima”) to act as Glacier’s representative in the construction of the project and to deliver the completed project to Glacier at a fixed price of approximately $104 million. Kajima has a service contract with Turner Construction Company, Inc. (“Turner Construction”) and is currently in the process of executing a construction contract with Turner for construction of the project for approximately
